CASES.titolo: A translation agency's website, built by translators
CASES.problema: A translation agency with over twenty years of history needed a site that spoke the language of the Italian B2B buyer: fast quotes, clear services, instant trust. The old site held up on neither load time nor service structure.
CASES.soluzione: We rebuilt the ATT (Technical Translation Agency) site as a complete business website: services architected by field and by language, a quote request in a few steps, copy written by the people who actually do the translating. Technical SEO and structured data from line one. It's our own calling card — if it didn't work, we'd be the first to know.
CASES.risultato: Live since 2022, the site brings the agency around 20 orders a month across more than 40 language pairs and directions. It's the channel ATT wins clients with today.
CASES.alt: Home page of the translation agency site traduzione.tech, services in focus
CASES.cta_testo: A business website like this one

CASES.titolo: The web app that runs a translation agency
CASES.problema: Running hundreds of translation orders — clients, translators, deadlines, quotes, invoices — on spreadsheets and email is a bottleneck that breaks deliveries. We needed one system, not ten disconnected tools.
CASES.soluzione: We built a full TMS (Translation Management System): a dashboard where every order has a status, every client a record, every translator a workload, and every job its invoice. Order board, contacts, quotes and accounting in a single web app. It's the group's internal operating system — we use it every day so no delivery slips through. This is our flagship: the same engineering we put into custom web apps for you.
CASES.risultato: In production for 2 years: the system handles 180 orders a month, over 2,000 a year. Inside it work 2 administrators, 8 project managers and 4 partner agencies with their own client base — and orders no longer get lost in email.
CASES.alt: Dashboard of the TMS at tms.perevod4.ru showing the translation order board
CASES.cta_testo: A custom web app for your company

shots.caption: TMS — order board, main screen, desktop

CASES.titolo: The Telegram Mini App almost nobody in Italy builds yet
CASES.problema: Translators and clients live inside chats, not at desks. Opening a browser dashboard just to check an order is too much. We needed access to the TMS inside Telegram, with nothing to install.
CASES.soluzione: We brought the TMS's key functions into a Telegram Mini App: orders, statuses and notifications right in the chat, with the same logic as the web dashboard. Telegram Mini Apps are still rare among Italian SMBs — exactly the kind of product we know how to build when the interface has to live where people already are.
CASES.risultato: Built in 2 weeks, the Mini App now handles over 10 users and orders a day: the back office in your pocket, nothing to install.
CASES.alt: Telegram Mini App of the TMS open inside the chat, showing the order list
CASES.cta_testo: A Mini App or PWA for your audience

shots.caption: Telegram Mini App — order list, mobile

CASES.titolo: A multilingual AI project, Italian included
CASES.problema: An AI-powered project only matters if the right person finds it, in their own language. Building a multilingual AI site whose technical SEO holds up in every version — Italian included — is engineering, not a translation plugin.
CASES.soluzione: We built ai.perevod4.ru as a multilingual project with a native Italian version: a clean technical structure, structured data, and content written for search in each language rather than machine-translated. The Italian version exists because we can serve the Italian market from the technical base up to the language.
CASES.risultato: Over 60 working languages and more than 10,000 requests processed a month: an AI project that gets found — and used — across markets.
CASES.alt: Italian version of the multilingual AI project ai.perevod4.ru

CASES.titolo: The catalogue that indexes an entire industry
CASES.problema: A catalogue portal covering an entire industry — agencies, cities, specialisms — lives or dies on technical SEO: if the structure doesn't hold, Google won't index it and the content doesn't exist.
CASES.soluzione: We built perevod4.ru as an editorial/SEO catalogue portal: translation agencies listed by city and specialism, with a consistent structure, structured data and speed kept under control across the whole volume. It's proof we can make technical SEO hold when the pages number in the hundreds, not the dozens.
CASES.risultato: Over 200 translation agencies listed in the catalogue: an entire industry made navigable and indexable, with technical SEO tested at scale, not on a demo.
CASES.alt: perevod4.ru catalogue portal listing translation agencies by city

CASES.titolo: A game to talk about translation (in Italian)
CASES.problema: A "serious" B2B service like translation struggles to be memorable. We wanted a way to keep people on the site and tell our story without a boring brochure.
CASES.soluzione: We created "Translation Empire", a browser game in Italian inside the agency site: gamification in the service of marketing — content that entertains while it explains what we do. It's the creative side of the same team that handles your redesign: when the job is to keep the user around, we know how to entertain them too.
CASES.risultato: Content that keeps people on the site and gives the brand something to talk about: gamification used as a marketing tool, not a gadget.
CASES.alt: Screen of the browser game “Translation Empire” in Italian on traduzione.tech
CASES.cta_testo: Redesign and marketing ideas for your site

CASES.titolo: A two-letter domain, a masterclass in technical SEO
CASES.problema: A very short domain on a non-Latin alphabet (IDN/punycode) is an edge case: ranking it well takes flawless technical SEO, because you can't lean on the name.
CASES.soluzione: We took пере.рф to the top positions with purely technical work: structure, speed, structured data, correct handling of the internationalised domain. It's one of our strongest SEO cases precisely because the result comes from engineering, not branding.
CASES.risultato: Position no. 1 on Yandex — Russia's leading search engine — for the industry queries "notarised translation" and "manual translation", 9,000–10,000 visitors a month, at the top for over 1,000 days. Top positions won with technique, not with the name.
CASES.alt: The пере.рф site, a technical SEO case on an internationalised domain

CASES.titolo: A brochure site for a small hospitality business
CASES.problema: A guest house lives on direct bookings and trust: a small tourism operator needs a simple, fast, credible site — not a back-office system.
CASES.soluzione: We built ukrinitsy.ru as an essential brochure site: photos that sell the place, contact and booking request within reach, fast loading even on mobile. The same "small but done right" approach we bring to restaurants, studios and local businesses.
CASES.risultato: Bookings grew 450% after launch: 18 bookings in a season straight from the site — for a small guest house, the difference between a full season and empty rooms.
CASES.alt: Brochure site of the ukrinitsy.ru guest house, mobile version
CASES.cta_testo: A brochure site for your business

CASES.titolo: Two "beautiful and fast" agency sites for a demanding market
CASES.problema: In the competitive translation-agency market a site has to be both beautiful and very fast: looks build trust, speed keeps it. Pulling that off on two different sites, at the same quality, is a test of method.
CASES.soluzione: We designed moscowtrans.ru and techperevod.com as a pair of agency sites — careful in design, optimised in performance: clear visual hierarchy, light images, a service structure built for conversion. It's our "design + redesign" side applied to an industry where competitors all look alike.
CASES.risultato: After the 2026 redesign of both look and functionality, conversions grew by over 300% in recent months: two sites that stand out in a sector where everyone looks alike.
CASES.alt: Home pages of the agency sites moscowtrans.ru and techperevod.com side by side
CASES.cta_testo: Redesign and new design for your site

CASES.titolo: The platform that decides who translates your copy
CASES.problema: The "native editors by specialism" promise you read on our service pages only holds if there's a serious way to select who translates. Picking translators by gut feeling is not a method.
CASES.soluzione: We built a translator testing and evaluation platform — a progressive selection system used across every project in the group: tests of increasing difficulty, structured scoring, automatic candidate filtering. It's the internal product that holds our translation quality: this is exactly the system behind the "native editors by profile" guarantee on the multilingual sites we deliver.
CASES.risultato: Over 400 translators tested — and only 8% pass the selection. Every translator who works on your copy is inside that 8%: quality decided by a system, not a hunch.
CASES.alt: Remarka group’s translator testing and evaluation platform
CASES.cta_testo: A custom web app for your process

CASES.titolo: An international project, written in English for the world
CASES.problema: A project aimed at a global audience lives or dies on language and search: English copy a native speaker reads as their own, and a technical base Google indexes without friction. Translating isn't enough — you have to write for that audience.
CASES.soluzione: We built 1russian.com as an international, English-language project: content written for people who search in English, not translated from Russian, with structure and technical SEO handled from the ground up (WordPress with a controlled deploy pipeline). It's also the proving ground for our in-house SEO audit tooling: we test on our own project first what we then apply to yours.
CASES.risultato: Over 10,000 visitors a month from 80 countries: a project that speaks English to a genuinely global audience.
CASES.alt: Home page of the international English-language project 1russian.com
CASES.cta_testo: Native content in several languages for your site

CITY_MILANO.sub: Progressive websites for SMBs in Milan and its province: PageSpeed 90+ guaranteed by contract, fixed delivery date, fixed price. The first meeting — at your office or ours — is free.
CITY_MILANO.case_eyebrow: The same engineering, for an Italian client
recensioni: Clear quote, delivered on the day promised. The site loads instantly even on the job site, where the signal is what it is.
Marco T. — construction company, Sesto S. Giovanni
They showed us the numbers on our old site before talking money. No one had ever done that.
Impeccable German version: our Munich clients now order from the site without emailing us for clarification.
Giulia B. — precision machining shop, Cinisello
faq: How much does a website cost in Milan?

Milan agencies charge an average of 2.500–8.000 € for a business website. Our prices are public: brochure sites from € 1.900, business websites from € 3.900, e-commerce from € 7.500 — fixed in the quote, with PageSpeed 90+ and the delivery date written into the contract.
Do you only work in the city of Milan?
No: Milan and the whole province, plus Monza and Brianza. The first meeting is free, at your office or at our studio in the Solari district.
Do we need to meet in person?
It’s not required: analysis, quote, and progress reviews all run through video calls and an online staging environment. But if you’re in Milan, the coffee is on us.

EXPORT_READY.hero_title: Your website works in German too. Measured, guaranteed, signed
EXPORT_READY.hero_sub: Your site and its international version under a single contract: native-speaker localization, international SEO, and KPIs for every market. In the language business since 2001.
EXPORT_READY.hero_stat_label: Languages with in-house native translators: English, German, French, Russian.
EXPORT_READY.problema_heading: The gap where exports lose money
EXPORT_READY.problema_testo: Exporters today choose between a web agency (which hands translations off to third parties or a plugin) and a translation agency (which builds neither websites nor international SEO). Responsibility always splits down the middle — exactly where your foreign customer decides whether to buy. Remarka is the only setup where engineering and professional localization live inside the same company, under the same contract.
EXPORT_READY.garanzie_heading: Export guarantees, in black and white
garanzie: Localization by native speakers only: translators’ names and credentials in the contract
A glossary of your industry’s terminology: it’s a deliverable, and it stays with you
Signed international SEO checklist: hreflang, metadata, and URLs for every market, attached to the acceptance report
Monthly KPIs for every market, for 12 months: traffic, rankings, inquiries
formati: One market, one language: target-market analysis by a native speaker, multilingual architecture, full localization, international SEO, 12 months of KPIs.
Several markets in a planned sequence: same technical foundation, shared glossary, market priorities set by the data, monthly comparative report.
processo: A native speaker studies how your customers search in their own country: words, competitors, prices.
URLs, hreflang, x-default, per-market metadata: the technical foundation Google requires.
Offer, CTAs, and forms adapted to the market, with a glossary approved with you.
Three-level QA (visual, functional, SEO), then 12 months of monthly KPIs per market.
faq: Why isn’t a translation plugin enough?

A plugin translates words, not selling points. A German customer spots machine-generated copy by the second line — and trust leaves with them. Our native speakers are in the contract, with name and credentials.
Do you really have translators in-house?
Yes: Studio Remarka is part of the Remarka group, in the language business since 2001, with in-house native translators for English, German, French, and Russian.
How do you measure results per market?
Each market gets its own monthly report: rankings on local keywords, organic traffic, form inquiries. For 12 months, included in the price.

WEB_APP.hero_title: When a website isn’t enough: web apps, client areas, configurators
WEB_APP.hero_sub: B2B portals, client dashboards, product configurators, CRM and ERP integrations. Built by the same team that develops the Remarka group’s own digital products.

per_chi: Companies managing orders, price lists, or case files by email that want a client area.
Manufacturers with configurable products: a configurator keeps selling while the technical office sleeps.
SMBs that need to connect website, CRM, and ERP without re-entering data by hand.
formati: Fixed scope, fixed price, fixed date: the first working version of your product in 6–10 weeks, ready for real users.
Iterative development with a shared backlog and regular releases: for products that grow with the business.
WEB_APP.prove_heading: The proof: our own internal products
WEB_APP.prove_testo: Not just for clients: the Remarka group builds and runs its own digital products in production — an AI translation platform, a TMS for language projects, and a standard-documents service. We publish them as case studies with real technical metrics, under the “Product Lab” label.
faq: How much does a web app cost?
The MVP Sprint format starts at € 15.000, with scope, price, and date fixed in the contract. Iterative projects start at € 25.000. The quote stays fixed: what you sign is what you pay.
Web app or native app?

For most SMBs, a web app (or PWA) costs a fraction of a native app, skips the app stores, and updates in a single release. A native app only makes sense for specific hardware or advanced notifications.
Who maintains the product after launch?
Twelve months of support included, same as our websites. After that, an optional plan or a full handover: code and documentation are yours from day one.

BLOG_POSTS.titolo: A website in four languages: costs, timelines, and the mistakes to avoid
BLOG_POSTS.estratto: When machine translation is enough, and when it costs you customers. With real per-language prices and a real case from the Remarka group.
BLOG_POSTS.corpo: Machine translation is fine for a menu or opening hours. It is not fine for a technical product page or a sales page, where one slip in register costs you a customer before they even write. In this article we explain when machine translation makes sense, when you need a native speaker, and what really changes in per-language costs and timelines, with a real case from the Remarka group.

BLOG_POSTS.titolo: Compliant cookie banners: the Garante’s 2026 checklist
BLOG_POSTS.estratto: “Reject” as prominent as “Accept,” no cookie walls, provable consent. What to check on your website, point by point.
BLOG_POSTS.corpo: The Italian Data Protection Authority (Garante) requires the “Reject” button in a cookie banner to carry the same visual weight as “Accept,” and consent to be provable over time. Simple rules — yet ignored by most of the Italian websites we analyze. Here is the point-by-point checklist we use to audit a site, and how we build our own banners to be compliant from day one.

BLOG_POSTS.titolo: Migrating off WordPress without losing your Google rankings
BLOG_POSTS.estratto: Redirects, URL structure, and what happens in the first six weeks. The protocol we use on every migration.
BLOG_POSTS.corpo: Every technical migration risks throwing away years of organic rankings. In this article we walk through the audit, URL-mapping, and 301-redirect protocol we apply before every WordPress migration, what we monitor in the first six weeks after the switch, and a real case where rankings held steady.

BLOG_POSTS.titolo: PWAs for SMBs: when you don’t need an app
BLOG_POSTS.estratto: A progressive website installs, works offline, and costs a fifth of a native app. The three cases where it truly pays off.
BLOG_POSTS.corpo: Building a native app costs on average 15.000–30.000 euros and requires separate maintenance for iOS and Android. A progressive website (PWA) delivers installability, notifications, and offline use at a fraction of the cost, with no app store review. In this article, the three concrete cases where a PWA truly pays off for an Italian SMB — and the two where a native app is still the right call.

BLOG_POSTS.titolo: What a business website costs in Italy: the real prices
BLOG_POSTS.estratto: From 800 to 50.000 euros: what actually changes between price tiers, and the questions to ask before signing any quote.
BLOG_POSTS.corpo: The Italian website market is fragmented and far from transparent: prices run from 800 euros for DIY site builders to 50.000 euros at large agencies. In this article we map, honestly, what you get at every price tier — including ours — and the questions to ask before signing any quote.

BLOG_POSTS.titolo: Core Web Vitals in 2026: what Google actually measures
BLOG_POSTS.estratto: LCP, INP, and CLS explained with examples from stores and workshops, not startups. And why the desktop score barely matters.
BLOG_POSTS.corpo: LCP, INP, and CLS are the three metrics Google uses to judge a site’s user experience — and to decide who shows up first in mobile search results. In this article we explain them without technical jargon, with real examples from stores and workshops — not startups — and why the desktop score many agencies still focus on barely matters anymore.

BLOG_POSTS.titolo: What an e-commerce site costs in Italy in 2026: the real numbers
BLOG_POSTS.estratto: From 6.000 to 25.000 euros and up: what actually changes between tiers, the recurring costs no one puts in the quote, and the questions to ask before signing.
BLOG_POSTS.corpo: “How much does an e-commerce site cost?” is how almost every first phone call with us begins. The honest answer is a wide range — 6.000 to 25.000 euros and up on the Italian market — because two online stores with the same number of products can hide completely different levels of complexity. In this article we lay out the real 2026 market figures, the costs that quotes keep quiet about, and the three factors that actually move the price.
cta: See our e-commerce price list — fixed prices

paragrafi: Public price lists from Italian agencies in 2026 describe a market in three tiers. Under 3.000 euros you get stores assembled on templates with minimal configuration: they work until you need to customize the checkout or connect your ERP. The middle tier — 6.000–15.000 euros — covers a professional, custom-built e-commerce site: a structured catalog, multiple payment methods, technical SEO on product pages. Above 15.000 you move into complex catalogs, ERP and logistics integrations, multiple languages and currencies.

Our price list sits in the middle tier — € 7.500–14.000, fixed in the quote — with one contractual difference: PageSpeed 90+ guaranteed in writing and a delivery date backed by a penalty clause. Those are the two items you will almost never find in competing offers in black and white.

The build price is half the story. A live e-commerce site costs money every year: hosting sized for traffic peaks, security updates, payment fees (typically 1,5–3% per transaction), e-invoicing, and catalog maintenance. On the market, professional maintenance runs between 500 and 2.000 euros a year: always ask what is included, and for how long.

In our case, the first 12 months of support, updates, and monthly measurements are included in the build price. After that, the plan is optional — or the store stays with you as is: code and data are yours from day one.

sezioni.titolo: The three factors that move the price
lista: Catalog: not how many products you have, but how many variants, price lists, and pricing rules (B2B vs B2C) the store has to handle.
Integrations: ERP, carriers, external warehouses. Every connected system is development and testing work, not a box to check.
Languages and markets: selling in Germany isn’t translating product pages — it’s VAT, shipping, returns, and copy a native speaker has to adapt to the market.
paragrafi: A catalog translated by native speakers and a checkout cut down to one step are the two levers that move the most direct sales in an e-commerce site selling abroad: we apply them to the same multilingual projects the Remarka group builds for itself — real cases, with a link to the live project, at /en/case-studies/.

sezioni.titolo: The questions to ask before signing
lista: Is the price fixed or “indicative”? What happens if extra work comes up?
Is the delivery date in the contract? With what penalty?
Who owns the domain, code, and customer data after delivery?
Is e-invoicing via SDI included, or is it a separate module?
How fast does the site load on mobile — and who guarantees it, in writing?

BLOG_POSTS.titolo: Slow website: the 7 real causes (and what fixing them costs)
BLOG_POSTS.estratto: Why a site still takes 5 seconds to load in 2026, which fixes cost little and return a lot, and when rebuilding the technical foundation is the better call.
BLOG_POSTS.corpo: Four seconds of load time on mobile sounds like a technical detail. It isn’t: it’s the reason your campaigns bring visits that leave before reading the first line. After dozens of audits on Italian SMB websites, the causes of slowness are almost always the same seven — and not all of them are expensive to fix. Here they are, in order of frequency, with the order of magnitude of the fix.
cta: Measure your site now — free test

sezioni.titolo: The seven causes, most frequent first
lista: Unoptimized images: 3–8 MB photos uploaded straight from the phone. The no. 1 cause, and the cheapest to fix.
Inadequate hosting: overcrowded shared servers that take 1–2 seconds to respond before the page even starts loading.
Bloated theme or template: page builders loading 2 MB of code to display three columns of text.
Plugins piled up over the years: every active plugin adds code, requests, and security risk — we have counted up to 60 on a single site.
External fonts and scripts: six font families and ten tracking services blocking the first paint.
No caching configured: the server rebuilds every page from scratch, for every visitor, every time.
An aging technical foundation: obsolete PHP versions and never-optimized databases — at this point, no single fix is enough.

sezioni.titolo: What costs little and returns a lot
paragrafi: The first two causes — images and hosting — can often be fixed within a day and can halve load times on their own. Modern compression (AVIF/WebP with progressive loading) cuts photo weight by 80% with no visible loss of quality: on its own, it's often the single optimisation with the highest cost-to-benefit ratio in an audit.
Caching and plugin cleanup are the second step: half a day’s work, measurable results right away. The point is to measure before and after — not “the site feels faster,” but a documented number.

sezioni.titolo: When a technical rebuild actually pays off
paragrafi: If your site combines causes 3, 4, and 7 — heavy theme, layered plugins, aging foundation — optimizing piece by piece is like putting new tires on a blown engine: every fix costs money and the result stays mediocre. In these cases a technical rebuild (same content, new foundation, 301 redirects so rankings are not lost) starts at € 2.900 and takes the site above 90 on PageSpeed — guaranteed by contract, not promised.
Either way, the first step is free: measure your site with our speed test, or request the full analysis — a written report with causes, priorities, and a fixed-price quote.
